title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: Functional Cox Regression (FCR) model extends classical Cox proportional hazards model settings one predictors functional (.e., curves trajectories observed continuum). subject =1,…,ni = 1, \ldots, n, let TiT_i event time CiC_i censoring time. observed data consist [Yi,δi,𝐙,{Wi(tm),tm∈𝒯}][Y_i, \delta_i, \mathbf{Z}_i, \{W_i(t_m), t_m \\mathcal{T}\}], : Yi=min(Ti,Ci)Y_i = \min(T_i, C_i) observed follow-time, δi\delta_i censoring indicator δi=0\delta_i = 0 event observed (Yi=TiY_i = T_i) δi=1\delta_i = 1 event censored (Yi<TiY_i < T_i), 𝐙\mathbf{Z}_i p×1p \times 1 vector scalar predictors, {Wi(tm),tm∈𝒯}\{W_i(t_m), t_m \\mathcal{T}\} m=1,…,Mm = 1, \ldots, M functional predictor observed domain 𝒯\mathcal{T}. domain 𝒯\mathcal{T} restricted [0,1][0,1]; determined actual time points data (e.g., 𝒯=[1,1440]\mathcal{T} = [1, 1440] minute-level 24-hour activity data). See section tmat, lmat, wmat details. model assumes proportional hazards structure: hi(t)=h0(t)exp(ηi)h_i(t) = h_0(t) \exp(\eta_i) h0(t)h_0(t) baseline hazard function, ηi\eta_i linear predictor defined : ηi=η0+∫𝒯Wi(s)β(s)ds+𝐙𝛄\eta_i = \eta_0 + \int_{\mathcal{T}} W_i(s)\beta(s)\,ds + \mathbf{Z}_i^t \boldsymbol{\gamma} η0\eta_0 intercept, β(⋅)\beta(\cdot) functional coefficient, 𝛄\boldsymbol{\gamma} vector scalar regression coefficients. integral approximated Riemann sum using time point matrix tmat integration weight matrix lmat (see ). log-likelihood model form: ℓ(𝐘,𝛅;h0,𝛈)=∑=1n[(1−δi){logh0(yi)+ηi−H0(yi)exp(ηi)}+δi{−H0(yi)exp(ηi)}]\ell(\mathbf{Y}, \boldsymbol{\delta}; h_0, \boldsymbol{\eta}) = \sum_{=1}^n \left[ (1 - \delta_i)\left\{\log h_0(y_i) + \eta_i - H_0(y_i)\exp(\eta_i)\right\} + \delta_i\left\{-H_0(y_i)\exp(\eta_i)\right\} \right] H0(t)=∫0th0(u)duH_0(t) = \int_0^t h_0(u)\,du cumulative baseline hazard function.

what: Modeling the Baseline Hazard with M-Splines
title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: Unlike classical Cox model, leaves baseline hazard unspecified uses partial likelihood, Bayesian approach requires full likelihood specification. fcox_bayes() function models baseline hazard function h0(t)h_0(t) using M-splines: h0(t)=∑l=1LclMl(t;𝐤,τ)h_0(t) = \sum_{l=1}^L c_l M_l(t; \mathbf{k}, \tau) Ml(t;𝐤,τ)M_l(t; \mathbf{k}, \tau) denotes ll-th M-spline basis function knots 𝐤\mathbf{k} degree τ\tau, 𝐜=(c1,…,cL)t\mathbf{c} = (c_1, \ldots, c_L)^t spline coefficients. constraints cl≥0c_l \geq 0 ∑l=1Lcl>0\sum_{l=1}^L c_l > 0 ensure baseline hazard non-negative non-trivial. M-splines family non-negative, piecewise polynomial basis functions integrate one support. cumulative baseline hazard function modeled using -splines, integrated forms M-splines: H0(t)=∑l=1LclIl(t;𝐤,τ)H_0(t) = \sum_{l=1}^L c_l I_l(t; \mathbf{k}, \tau) Il(t;𝐤,τ)=∫0tMl(u;𝐤,τ)duI_l(t; \mathbf{k}, \tau) = \int_0^t M_l(u; \mathbf{k}, \tau)\,du. M-splines non-negative, resulting -spline cumulative hazard non-decreasing construction.

Consequently, intercept defaults FALSE fcox_bayes().

text: complete Bayesian Functional Cox Regression model : {𝐘∼ℓ(𝐘,𝛅;h0,𝛈)𝛈=η0𝐉n+𝐗̃rt𝐛̃r+𝐗̃ft𝐛̃f+𝐙t𝛄𝐛̃r∼N(𝟎,σb2𝐈)η0∼p(η0);𝐛̃f∼p(𝐛̃f);𝛄∼p(𝛄);σb2∼p(σb2)h0(t)=∑l=1LclMl(t;𝐤,τ)𝐜∼D(𝐜;𝛂)\begin{cases} \mathbf{Y} \sim \ell(\mathbf{Y}, \boldsymbol{\delta}; h_0, \boldsymbol{\eta}) \\ \boldsymbol{\eta} = \eta_0 \mathbf{J}_n + \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_r^t \tilde{\mathbf{b}}_r + \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_f^t \tilde{\mathbf{b}}_f + \mathbf{Z}^t \boldsymbol{\gamma} \\ \tilde{\mathbf{b}}_r \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma_b^2 \mathbf{}) \\ \eta_0 \sim p(\eta_0);\; \tilde{\mathbf{b}}_f \sim p(\tilde{\mathbf{b}}_f);\; \boldsymbol{\gamma} \sim p(\boldsymbol{\gamma});\; \sigma_b^2 \sim p(\sigma_b^2) \\ h_0(t) = \sum_{l=1}^L c_l M_l(t; \mathbf{k}, \tau) \\ \mathbf{c} \sim D(\mathbf{c}; \boldsymbol{\alpha}) \end{cases} components shared SoFR model, except Cox likelihood (first line) baseline hazard specification via M-splines Dirichlet prior 𝐜\mathbf{c} (last two lines).

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: demonstrate fcox_bayes() function using simulated example dataset time--event outcome one functional predictor.

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: example dataset contains: survtime: observed survival time Yi=min(Ti,Ci)Y_i = \min(T_i, C_i), event: binary event indicator (11 = event occurred, 00 = censored), X1: scalar predictor, tmat: n×Mn \times M time point matrix (defines domain 𝒯\mathcal{T}), lmat: n×Mn \times M Riemann sum weight matrix (defines integration weights 𝒯\mathcal{T}), MIMS: n×Mn \times M functional predictor matrix (physical activity data). Note censoring indicator cens constructed 1 - event, following convention δi=0\delta_i = 0 indicates observed event δi=1\delta_i = 1 indicates censoring.
code: ## Load the example data Func_Cox_Data <- readRDS("data/example_data_Cox.rds")  ## Prepare the functional predictor and censoring indicator Func_Cox_Data$wmat <- Func_Cox_Data$MIMS Func_Cox_Data$cens <- 1 - Func_Cox_Data$event

what: Fit the Bayesian Functional Cox Model
title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: call: formula specifies observed survival time survtime response, one scalar predictor X1 one functional predictor encoded via s(tmat, = lmat * wmat, bs = "cc", k = 10). spline basis evaluated time points stored tmat, integration domain 𝒯\mathcal{T} (determined tmat) approximated using weights lmat. censoring vector cens supplied separately, 00 = event observed 11 = censored. bs = "cc" uses cyclic cubic regression splines, appropriate periodic functional predictors (e.g., 24-hour activity patterns). k = 10 specifies 10 basis functions. practice, 30–40 basis functions recommended moderately smooth functional data dense grids. intercept argument specified defaults FALSE, appropriate Cox models due identifiability constraint baseline hazard. sampler runs 3 chains parallel, 1500 total iterations (500 warmup + 1000 posterior samples).
code: library(refundBayes)  refundBayes_FCox <- refundBayes::fcox_bayes(   survtime ~ X1 + s(tmat, by = lmat * wmat, bs = "cc", k = 10),   data = Func_Cox_Data,   cens = Func_Cox_Data$cens,   runStan = TRUE,   niter = 1500,   nwarmup = 500,   nchain = 3,   ncores = 3 )

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: plot() method refundBayes objects displays estimated functional coefficient β̂(t)\hat{\beta}(t) along pointwise 95% credible intervals:
code: library(ggplot2) plot(refundBayes_FCox)

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: Posterior summaries functional coefficient can computed directly func_coef element: posterior samples func_coef[[1]] stored Q×MQ \times M matrix, QQ number posterior samples MM number time points functional domain.
code: ## Posterior mean of the functional coefficient mean_curve <- apply(refundBayes_FCox$func_coef[[1]], 2, mean)  ## Pointwise 95% credible interval upper_curve <- apply(refundBayes_FCox$func_coef[[1]], 2,                      function(x) quantile(x, prob = 0.975)) lower_curve <- apply(refundBayes_FCox$func_coef[[1]], 2,                      function(x) quantile(x, prob = 0.025))

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: Bayesian results can compared frequentist estimates obtained via mgcv::gam, handles Cox proportional hazards models cox.ph() family using partial likelihood: Note frequentist approach uses cox.ph() family partial likelihood Breslow-type nonparametric estimator baseline hazard, Bayesian approach models baseline hazard parametrically using M-splines. Despite difference, simulation studies Jiang et al. (2025) show approaches achieve comparable performance terms estimation accuracy coverage.
code: library(mgcv)  ## Fit frequentist functional Cox model using mgcv fit_freq <- gam(   survtime ~ s(tmat, by = lmat * wmat, bs = "cc", k = 10) + X1,   data = Func_Cox_Data,   family = cox.ph(),   weights = Func_Cox_Data$event )  ## Extract frequentist estimates freq_result <- plot(fit_freq)

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: Setting runStan = FALSE allows inspect modify Stan code running model: generated Stan code includes functions block defines custom log-likelihood functions Cox model (cox_log_lpdf observed events cox_log_lccdf censored observations), addition standard data, parameters, model blocks.
code: ## Generate Stan code without running the sampler fcox_code <- refundBayes::fcox_bayes(   survtime ~ X1 + s(tmat, by = lmat * wmat, bs = "cc", k = 10),   data = Func_Cox_Data,   cens = Func_Cox_Data$cens,   runStan = FALSE )  ## Print the generated Stan code cat(fcox_code$stancode)

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression
text: Number basis functions (k): illustrative purposes, k = 10 often used. practice, 30–40 basis functions recommended moderately smooth functional data observed dense grids. Spline type (bs): Use "cr" (cubic regression splines) general functional predictors. Use "cc" (cyclic cubic regression splines) functional predictor periodic (e.g., 24-hour activity patterns). Intercept: intercept defaults FALSE Cox models simultaneously identifiable baseline hazard M-spline coefficients. cases, default changed. Censoring vector: Ensure cens vector correctly follows convention δi=0\delta_i = 0 observed events δi=1\delta_i = 1 censored observations. data event indicator (1 = event, 0 = censored), use cens = 1 - event. Number iterations: Cox models may require iterations standard SoFR models. common setup niter = 3000 nwarmup = 1000. Watch divergent transitions Stan output; occur, consider increasing number warmup iterations adjusting adapt_delta parameter. Convergence diagnostics: fitting, examine traceplots R̂\hat{R} statistics using rstan package (e.g., rstan::traceplot(refundBayes_FCox$stanfit)) ensure Markov chains converged. Joint FPCA: functional predictors measured substantial noise, consider setting joint_FPCA = TRUE relevant predictor jointly estimate FPCA scores regression coefficients within Cox model. See Joint FPCA vignette details.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression
text: vignette provides detailed guide fofr_bayes() function refundBayes package, fits Bayesian Function--Function Regression (FoFR) models using Stan. FoFR extends scalar--function regression (SoFR) function--scalar regression (FoSR) frameworks modeling functional response function one functional predictors, along optional scalar covariates. contrast SoFR, outcome scalar predictors functional, FoSR, outcome functional predictors scalar, FoFR allows effect functional predictor functional response captured bivariate coefficient function β(s,t)\beta(s, t). model specified using mgcv-style syntax regression functions refundBayes package. methodology extends framework described Jiang, Crainiceanu, Cui (2025), Tutorial Bayesian Functional Regression Using Stan, published Statistics Medicine.

Let 𝐗=(Xi1,…,XiP)t\mathbf{X}_i = (X_{i1}, \ldots, X_{iP})^t P×1P \times 1 vector scalar predictors (first covariate may intercept, Xi1=1X_{i1} = 1). FoFR model assumes: Yi(t)=∑p=1PXipαp(t)+∫𝒮Wi(s)β(s,t)ds+ei(t)Y_i(t) = \sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip}\,\alpha_p(t) + \int_{\mathcal{S}} W_i(s)\,\beta(s, t)\,ds + e_i(t) : αp(t)\alpha_p(t) functional coefficients scalar predictors, describing pp-th scalar predictor affects response point t∈𝒯t \\mathcal{T}, β(s,t)\beta(s, t) bivariate coefficient function characterizes functional predictor predictor-domain location ss affects response response-domain location tt, ei(t)e_i(t) residual process, correlated across tt. integral ∫𝒮Wi(s)β(s,t)ds\int_{\mathcal{S}} W_i(s)\,\beta(s, t)\,ds approximated using Riemann sum observed predictor-domain grid points. domains 𝒮\mathcal{S} 𝒯\mathcal{T} restricted [0,1][0,1]; determined actual observation grids data. multiple functional predictors present, model extends naturally: Yi(t)=∑p=1PXipαp(t)+∑j=1J∫𝒮jWij(s)βj(s,t)ds+ei(t)Y_i(t) = \sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip}\,\alpha_p(t) + \sum_{j=1}^J \int_{\mathcal{S}_j} W_{ij}(s)\,\beta_j(s, t)\,ds + e_i(t) βj(s,t)\beta_j(s, t) bivariate coefficient function jj-th functional predictor.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression
text: account within-subject correlation residuals, model decomposes ei(t)e_i(t) using functional principal components, following approach FoSR (Goldsmith, Zipunnikov, Schrack, 2015): ei(t)=∑r=1Rξirϕr(t)+ϵi(t)e_i(t) = \sum_{r=1}^R \xi_{ir}\,\phi_r(t) + \epsilon_i(t) ϕ1(t),…,ϕR(t)\phi_1(t), \ldots, \phi_R(t) eigenfunctions estimated via FPCA (using refund::fpca.face), ξir\xi_{ir} subject-specific FPCA scores ξir∼N(0,λr)\xi_{ir} \sim N(0, \lambda_r), ϵi(t)∼N(0,σϵ2)\epsilon_i(t) \sim N(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2) independent measurement error. eigenvalues λr\lambda_r error variance σϵ2\sigma_\epsilon^2 estimated data.

what: Bivariate Coefficient via Tensor Product Basis
title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression
text: key feature FoFR bivariate coefficient function β(s,t)\beta(s, t), lives product domain 𝒮×𝒯\mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{T}. function represented using tensor product two sets basis functions: Predictor-domain basis: B1(s),…,BQ(s)B_1(s), \ldots, B_Q(s), constructed s() term formula using mgcv spline basis (e.g., cubic regression splines). decomposed random fixed effect components via spectral reparametrisation mgcv::smooth2random(), yielding B̃1r(s),…,B̃Qrr(s)\tilde{B}_1^r(s), \ldots, \tilde{B}_{Q_r}^r(s) (random effects) B̃1f(s),…,B̃Qff(s)\tilde{B}_1^f(s), \ldots, \tilde{B}_{Q_f}^f(s) (fixed effects). Response-domain basis: ψ1(t),…,ψK(t)\psi_1(t), \ldots, \psi_K(t), spline basis used scalar predictor coefficients. bivariate coefficient : β(s,t)=∑k=1K[∑q=1QrθqkrB̃qr(s)+∑q=1QfθqkfB̃qf(s)]ψk(t)\beta(s, t) = \sum_{k=1}^K \left[\sum_{q=1}^{Q_r} \theta_{qk}^r\,\tilde{B}_q^r(s) + \sum_{q=1}^{Q_f} \theta_{qk}^f\,\tilde{B}_q^f(s)\right] \psi_k(t) 𝚯r\boldsymbol{\Theta}^r Qr×KQ_r \times K matrix random effect coefficients 𝚯f\boldsymbol{\Theta}^f Qf×KQ_f \times K matrix fixed effect coefficients. representation, integral contribution functional predictor becomes: ∫𝒮Wi(s)β(s,t)ds≈[𝐗̃ir𝚯r+𝐗̃𝚯f]𝛙(t)\int_{\mathcal{S}} W_i(s)\,\beta(s, t)\,ds \approx \left[\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_{}^r \boldsymbol{\Theta}^r + \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_{}^f \boldsymbol{\Theta}^f\right] \boldsymbol{\psi}(t) 𝐗̃ir\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_{}^r 𝐗̃\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_{}^f 1×Qr1 \times Q_r 1×Qf1 \times Q_f row vectors transformed predictor-domain design matrices subject ii (matrices used SoFR). matrix notation subjects, functional predictor contribution mean : (𝐗̃r𝚯r+𝐗̃f𝚯f)𝚿\left(\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^r \boldsymbol{\Theta}^r + \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^f \boldsymbol{\Theta}^f\right) \boldsymbol{\Psi} n×Mn \times M matrix, 𝚿\boldsymbol{\Psi} K×MK \times M matrix response-domain spline basis evaluations.

text: complete Bayesian FoFR model combines mean structure, residual FPCA decomposition, priors: {Yi(t)=𝛍(t)+ei(t),=1,…,n𝛍(t)=∑p=1PXipαp(t)+∫𝒮Wi(s)β(s,t)dsei(t)=∑r=1Rξirϕr(t)+ϵi(t)\begin{cases} Y_i(t) = \boldsymbol{\mu}_i(t) + e_i(t), \quad = 1, \ldots, n \\[6pt] \boldsymbol{\mu}_i(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip}\,\alpha_p(t) + \int_{\mathcal{S}} W_i(s)\,\beta(s,t)\,ds \\[6pt] e_i(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^R \xi_{ir}\,\phi_r(t) + \epsilon_i(t) \end{cases} matrix form subjects: 𝐘=𝐗𝐀t𝚿+(𝐗̃r𝚯r+𝐗̃f𝚯f)𝚿+𝚵𝚽+𝐄\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{}^t \boldsymbol{\Psi} + \left(\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^r \boldsymbol{\Theta}^r + \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^f \boldsymbol{\Theta}^f\right) \boldsymbol{\Psi} + \boldsymbol{\Xi}\,\boldsymbol{\Phi} + \boldsymbol{E} 𝐘\mathbf{Y} n×Mn \times M matrix functional responses, 𝐗\mathbf{X} n×Pn \times P scalar design matrix, 𝐀\mathbf{} K×PK \times P matrix scalar predictor spline coefficients, 𝚵\boldsymbol{\Xi} n×Rn \times R matrix FPCA scores, 𝚽\boldsymbol{\Phi} R×MR \times M matrix eigenfunctions.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression
text: formula combines FoSR syntax (functional response left-hand side) SoFR syntax (functional predictors via s() terms right-hand side): : Y_mat: name functional response variable data. n×Mn \times M matrix, row contains functional observations one subject across MM response-domain time points. X1, X2: scalar predictor(s), included using standard formula syntax. sindex: n×Ln \times L matrix predictor-domain grid points. row contains LL observation points (replicated across subjects). X_func: n×Ln \times L matrix functional predictor values. ii-th row contains LL observed values subject ii. bs: type spline basis predictor domain (e.g., "cr" cubic regression splines). k: number basis functions predictor domain. response-domain spline basis controlled separately via spline_type spline_df arguments fofr_bayes(). design separates two basis specifications: predictor-domain basis specified formula (SoFR), response-domain basis specified via function arguments (FoSR). Multiple functional predictors can included adding additional s() terms.
code: Y_mat ~ X1 + X2 + s(sindex, by = X_func, bs = "cr", k = 10)

title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression
text: simulated dataset dat contains: Y_mat: n×Mn \times M matrix functional response values, age: scalar predictor, X_func: n×Ln \times L matrix functional predictor values (smooth random curves), sindex: n×Ln \times L matrix predictor-domain grid points (identical rows). true data-generating model : Yi(t)=agei⋅0.5sin(πt)+1L∑l=1LXi(sl)sin(2πsl)cos(2πt)+ϵi(t),ϵi(t)∼N(0,0.32)Y_i(t) = \text{age}_i \cdot 0.5\sin(\pi t) + \frac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^L X_i(s_l)\,\sin(2\pi s_l)\cos(2\pi t) + \epsilon_i(t), \quad \epsilon_i(t) \sim N(0, 0.3^2)
code: library(refundBayes)  set.seed(42)  # --- Dimensions --- n  <- 200   # number of subjects L  <- 30    # number of predictor-domain grid points M  <- 30    # number of response-domain grid points  sindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = L)   # predictor domain grid tindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M)   # response domain grid  # --- Functional predictor X(s): smooth random curves --- X_func <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = L) for (i in 1:n) {   X_func[i, ] <- rnorm(1) * sin(2 * pi * sindex) +                  rnorm(1) * cos(2 * pi * sindex) +                  rnorm(1) * sin(4 * pi * sindex) +                  rnorm(1, sd = 0.3) }  # --- Scalar predictor --- age <- rnorm(n)  # --- True coefficient functions --- # Bivariate coefficient: beta(s, t) = sin(2*pi*s) * cos(2*pi*t) beta_true <- outer(sin(2 * pi * sindex), cos(2 * pi * tindex))  # Scalar coefficient function: alpha(t) = 0.5 * sin(pi*t) alpha_true <- 0.5 * sin(pi * tindex)  # --- Generate functional response --- # Y_i(t) = age_i * alpha(t) + integral X_i(s) beta(s,t) ds + epsilon_i(t) signal_scalar <- outer(age, alpha_true)                    # n x M signal_func   <- (X_func %*% beta_true) / L               # n x M  (Riemann sum) epsilon        <- matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = 0.3), nrow = n) # n x M  Y_mat <- signal_scalar + signal_func + epsilon  # --- Organize data --- dat <- data.frame(age = age) dat$Y_mat  <- Y_mat dat$X_func <- X_func dat$sindex <- matrix(rep(sindex, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE)

title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression
text: estimated bivariate coefficient β̂(s,t)\hat{\beta}(s,t) stored 3-d array bivar_func_coef. posterior mean surface comparison truth can visualised using heatmaps: richer 3-d surface visualisations, use fields::image.plot() plotly::plot_ly() type "surface".
code: # Posterior mean of the bivariate coefficient beta_est  <- apply(fit_fofr$bivar_func_coef[[1]], c(2, 3), mean)  # Pointwise 95% credible interval bounds beta_lower <- apply(fit_fofr$bivar_func_coef[[1]], c(2, 3),                     function(x) quantile(x, 0.025)) beta_upper <- apply(fit_fofr$bivar_func_coef[[1]], c(2, 3),                     function(x) quantile(x, 0.975))  # Side-by-side heatmaps: true vs estimated vs difference par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1)) image(sindex, tindex, beta_true,       xlab = "s (predictor domain)", ylab = "t (response domain)",       main = expression("True " * beta(s, t)),       col = hcl.colors(64, "Blue-Red 3")) image(sindex, tindex, beta_est,       xlab = "s (predictor domain)", ylab = "t (response domain)",       main = expression("Estimated " * hat(beta)(s, t)),       col = hcl.colors(64, "Blue-Red 3")) image(sindex, tindex, beta_est - beta_true,       xlab = "s (predictor domain)", ylab = "t (response domain)",       main = "Difference (Est - True)",       col = hcl.colors(64, "Blue-Red 3"))

code: alpha_est   <- apply(fit_fofr$scalar_func_coef[, 1, ], 2, mean) alpha_lower <- apply(fit_fofr$scalar_func_coef[, 1, ], 2,                      function(x) quantile(x, 0.025)) alpha_upper <- apply(fit_fofr$scalar_func_coef[, 1, ], 2,                      function(x) quantile(x, 0.975))  par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) plot(tindex, alpha_true, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "black",      ylim = range(c(alpha_lower, alpha_upper)),      xlab = "t (response domain)", ylab = expression(alpha(t)),      main = "Scalar coefficient function: age") lines(tindex, alpha_est, col = "blue", lwd = 2) polygon(c(tindex, rev(tindex)),         c(alpha_lower, rev(alpha_upper)),         col = rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.2), border = NA) legend("topright",        legend = c("Truth", "Posterior mean", "95% CI"),        col = c("black", "blue", rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.2)),        lwd = c(2, 2, 10), bty = "n")

text: vignette provides detailed guide fosr_bayes() function refundBayes package, fits Bayesian Function--Scalar Regression (FoSR) models using Stan. contrast scalar--function regression (SoFR), outcome scalar predictors include functional variables, FoSR reverses relationship: outcome functional variable (curve observed continuum) predictors scalar. methodology follows tutorial Jiang, Crainiceanu, Cui (2025), Tutorial Bayesian Functional Regression Using Stan, published Statistics Medicine. procedure fitting generalized multilevel Bayesian FoSR introduced Goldsmith, Zipunnikov, Schrack (2015).

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: refundBayes package can installed CRAN: latest version refundBayes package, users can install GitHub:
code: install.packages("refundBayes") library(remotes) remotes::install_github("https://github.com/ZirenJiang/refundBayes")

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: Function--Scalar Regression (FoSR) models relationship functional outcome one scalar predictors. Two key differences traditional regression : (1) outcome Yi(t)Y_i(t) multivariate often high-dimensional, (2) residuals ei(t)e_i(t) correlated across tt (points domain). subject =1,…,ni = 1, \ldots, n, let Yi(t)Y_i(t) functional response observed time points t=t1,…,tMt = t_1, \ldots, t_M domain 𝒯\mathcal{T}. domain 𝒯\mathcal{T} restricted [0,1][0,1]; determined actual time points data (e.g., 𝒯=[1,1440]\mathcal{T} = [1, 1440] minute-level 24-hour data). FoSR model assumes: Yi(t)=∑p=1PXipβp(t)+ei(t)Y_i(t) = \sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip} \beta_p(t) + e_i(t) : XipX_{ip} p=1,…,Pp = 1, \ldots, P scalar predictors (first covariate often intercept, Xi1=1X_{i1} = 1), βp(t)\beta_p(t) corresponding domain-varying (functional) coefficients, ei(t)e_i(t) residual process, correlated across tt. functional coefficient βp(t)\beta_p(t) describes pp-th scalar predictor affects outcome point tt domain. allows effect scalar predictor vary smoothly domain.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: account within-subject correlation residuals, model decomposes ei(t)e_i(t) using functional principal components: ei(t)=∑j=1Jξijϕj(t)+ϵi(t)e_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^J \xi_{ij} \phi_j(t) + \epsilon_i(t) ϕ1(t),…,ϕJ(t)\phi_1(t), \ldots, \phi_J(t) eigenfunctions estimated via FPCA (using refund::fpca.face), ξij\xi_{ij} subject-specific scores ξij∼N(0,λj)\xi_{ij} \sim N(0, \lambda_j), ϵi(t)∼N(0,σϵ2)\epsilon_i(t) \sim N(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2) independent measurement error. eigenvalues λj\lambda_j error variance σϵ2\sigma_\epsilon^2 estimated data.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: functional coefficient βp(t)\beta_p(t) represented using KK spline basis functions ψ1(t),…,ψK(t)\psi_1(t), \ldots, \psi_K(t): βp(t)=∑k=1Kbpkψk(t)\beta_p(t) = \sum_{k=1}^K b_{pk} \psi_k(t) Substituting model: Yi(t)=∑k=1K(∑p=1PXipbpk)ψk(t)+∑j=1Jξijϕj(t)+ϵi(t)Y_i(t) = \sum_{k=1}^K \left(\sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip} b_{pk}\right) \psi_k(t) + \sum_{j=1}^J \xi_{ij} \phi_j(t) + \epsilon_i(t) Let Bik=∑p=1PXipbpkB_{ik} = \sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip} b_{pk} denote 𝚿\boldsymbol{\Psi} M×KM \times K matrix spline basis evaluations, 𝚽\boldsymbol{\Phi} M×JM \times J matrix eigenfunctions, 𝐁=(Bi1,…,BiK)t\mathbf{B}_i = (B_{i1}, \ldots, B_{iK})^t. model matrix form becomes: 𝐘=𝚿𝐁+𝚽𝛏+𝛜\mathbf{Y}_i = \boldsymbol{\Psi} \mathbf{B}_i + \boldsymbol{\Phi} \boldsymbol{\xi}_i + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_i 𝐘={Yi(t1),…,Yi(tM)}t\mathbf{Y}_i = \{Y_i(t_1), \ldots, Y_i(t_M)\}^t M×1M \times 1 vector observed functional data subject ii.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: Smoothness βp(t)\beta_p(t) induced quadratic penalty spline coefficients 𝐛p=(bp1,…,bpK)t\mathbf{b}_p = (b_{p1}, \ldots, b_{pK})^t: p(𝐛p)∝exp(−𝐛pt𝐒𝐛p2σp2)p(\mathbf{b}_p) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{\mathbf{b}_p^t \mathbf{S} \mathbf{b}_p}{2\sigma_p^2}\right) 𝐒\mathbf{S} penalty matrix σp2\sigma_p^2 smoothing parameter pp-th functional coefficient. Importantly, functional coefficient βp(t)\beta_p(t) smoothing parameter σp2\sigma_p^2, allowing different levels smoothness across predictors. Unlike SoFR FCox models, FoSR implementation uses original spline basis without spectral reparametrisation. choice follows Goldsmith et al. (2015) simplifies interpretation avoiding need back-transform estimated coefficients.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: complete Bayesian FoSR model : {𝐘=𝚿𝐁+𝚽𝛏+𝛜iBik=∑p=1PXipbpk,k=1,…,Kp(𝐛p)∝exp(−𝐛pt𝐒𝐛p/2σp2),p=1,…,Pξij∼N(0,λj),λj∼p(λj),j=1,…,Jϵi(tm)∼N(0,σϵ2),=1,…,n,t=t1,…,tMσϵ2∼p(σϵ2),σp2∼p(σp2),p=1,…,P\begin{cases} \mathbf{Y}_i = \boldsymbol{\Psi} \mathbf{B}_i + \boldsymbol{\Phi} \boldsymbol{\xi}_i + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_i \\ B_{ik} = \sum_{p=1}^P X_{ip} b_{pk}, \quad k = 1, \ldots, K \\ p(\mathbf{b}_p) \propto \exp(-\mathbf{b}_p^t \mathbf{S} \mathbf{b}_p / 2\sigma_p^2), \quad p = 1, \ldots, P \\ \xi_{ij} \sim N(0, \lambda_j), \quad \lambda_j \sim p(\lambda_j), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J \\ \epsilon_i(t_m) \sim N(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2), \quad = 1, \ldots, n, \; t = t_1, \ldots, t_M \\ \sigma_\epsilon^2 \sim p(\sigma_\epsilon^2), \quad \sigma_p^2 \sim p(\sigma_p^2), \quad p = 1, \ldots, P \end{cases} priors p(λj)p(\lambda_j), p(σϵ2)p(\sigma_\epsilon^2), p(σp2)p(\sigma_p^2) non-informative priors variance components, using inverse-Gamma priors IG(0.001,0.001)IG(0.001, 0.001). model assumes functional responses observed common set grid points across subjects. ensures 𝚿\boldsymbol{\Psi} 𝚽\boldsymbol{\Phi} matrices identical across subjects, simplifying computation Stan.

text: call: formula specifies functional response y (n×Mn \times M matrix) one scalar predictor X. spline basis functional coefficients constructed internally using B-splines (spline_type = "bs") 10 degrees freedom (spline_df = 10) default. eigenfunctions residual structure estimated automatically via FPCA using refund::fpca.face. sampler runs 1 chain 1500 total iterations (500 warmup + 1000 posterior samples). production analyses, using 3 chains recommended convergence assessment.

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: Bayesian results can compared frequentist estimates obtained via mgcv::gam. described Crainiceanu et al. (2024), one key distinction frequentist approach fits model time point independently using fast algorithms, Bayesian approach jointly models time points shared smoothness prior FPCA-based residual structure: Simulation studies Jiang et al. (2025) show Bayesian FoSR achieves similar estimation accuracy (RISE) frequentist approach, providing superior coverage pointwise credible intervals.
code: library(refund)  ## The frequentist approach can be implemented using mgcv or refund::pffr ## See Crainiceanu et al. (2024) for details  fit.freq = pffr(y~-1+X,data=FoSR_exp_data,bs.yindex=list(bs="cc", k=10)) plotfot = plot(fit.freq)

title: Bayesian Function-on-Scalar Regression
text: Number basis functions (spline_df): default spline_df = 10. practice, appropriate number depends complexity underlying functional coefficients resolution data. basis functions allow greater flexibility increase computational cost. Spline type (spline_type): default "bs" uses B-splines. basis types supported mgcv may also used depending application. Number FPCA components: number eigenfunctions JJ used model residual correlation determined automatically refund::fpca.face. residual structure complex, components may needed. Number iterations chains: FoSR models computationally intensive. Start fewer iterations single chain exploratory analysis, increase final inference. recommended setup production niter = 3000, nwarmup = 1000, nchain = 3. Convergence diagnostics: fitting, examine traceplots R̂\hat{R} statistics using rstan package (e.g., rstan::traceplot(refundBayes_FoSR$stanfit)). Warnings bulk ESS, tail ESS, R̂\hat{R} indicate iterations chains may needed. Common grid assumption: current implementation assumes functional responses observed common set grid points across subjects. Subject-specific observation grids yet supported.

title: Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis
text: vignette provides detailed guide fpca_bayes() function refundBayes package, fits Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis (FPCA) model using Stan. Unlike regression-style functions package (sofr_bayes, fosr_bayes, fcox_bayes, fofr_bayes), fpca_bayes() relate functional response predictors — instead decomposes sample curves smooth population mean function plus small number subject-specific functional principal components, full Bayesian uncertainty quantification every quantity except eigenfunctions . methodology builds directly tutorial Jiang, Crainiceanu, Cui (2025), Tutorial Bayesian Functional Regression Using Stan, published Statistics Medicine, reuses penalized-spline + Stan machinery powers fosr_bayes() modelling mean function.

Stan model evaluates 𝐘=𝟏n(𝛂⊤𝚿)+𝛏𝚽+ℰ,ℰim∼iid𝒩(0,σϵ2),\mathbf{Y} \;=\; \mathbf{1}_n (\boldsymbol{\alpha}^\top \boldsymbol{\Psi}) \;+\; \boldsymbol{\xi}\, \boldsymbol{\Phi} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\mathcal{E}}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\mathcal{E}}_{im} \stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2), implemented

title: Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis
text: complete Bayesian FPCA model : {𝐘=𝚿⊤𝛂+𝚽⊤𝛏+𝛜,=1,…,n𝚽 fixed (plug-𝚛𝚎𝚏𝚞𝚗𝚍::𝚏𝚙𝚌𝚊.𝚜𝚌)p(𝛂∣σμ2)∝exp(−𝛂⊤𝐒𝛂/2σμ2)ξij∣λj∼𝒩(0,λj2),j=1,…,Jσμ2,λj2,σϵ2∼InvGamma(0.001,0.001)\begin{cases} \mathbf{Y}_i = \boldsymbol{\Psi}^\top \boldsymbol{\alpha} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\xi}_i + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_i,\quad = 1, \ldots, n \\[4pt] \boldsymbol{\Phi} \text{ fixed} \text{ (plug-} \texttt{refund::fpca.sc}\text{)} \\[4pt] p(\boldsymbol{\alpha} \mid \sigma_\mu^2) \propto \exp\!\big(- \boldsymbol{\alpha}^\top \mathbf{S}\, \boldsymbol{\alpha} \,/\, 2\sigma_\mu^2 \big) \\[4pt] \xi_{ij} \mid \lambda_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \lambda_j^2),\quad j = 1, \ldots, J \\[4pt] \sigma_\mu^2,\ \lambda_j^2,\ \sigma_\epsilon^2 \sim \mathrm{InvGamma}(0.001, 0.001) \end{cases} inverse-Gamma hyper-priors non-informative, exactly matching convention used sofr_bayes, fosr_bayes, fcox_bayes, fofr_bayes.

title: Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis
text: fpca_bayes() called, function performs following steps internally invoking Stan: Parse formula brms::brmsformula() extract response variable name. Validate data[[y_var]] exists matrix. Run initial frequentist FPCA via refund::fpca.sc(Y_mat, npc = npc) obtain eigenfunction basis 𝚽\boldsymbol{\Phi} number components JJ. Build mean-function spline basis 𝚿\boldsymbol{\Psi} penalty matrix 𝐒\mathbf{S} using mgcv::smooth.construct() dummy grid 1:M. penalty matrix rescaled smoothing variance numerically sensible scale. Assemble Stan data list containing NN, MM, JJ, KK, 𝐘\mathbf{Y}, 𝚽\boldsymbol{\Phi}, 𝚿\boldsymbol{\Psi}, 𝐒\mathbf{S}. Generate Stan code block (data, transformed data, parameters, transformed parameters, model) concatenate . Run Stan (skip runStan = FALSE) extract posterior samples. Reconstruct mean function input grid computing 𝛂⊤𝚿\boldsymbol{\alpha}^\top \boldsymbol{\Psi} every posterior draw 𝛂\boldsymbol{\alpha}, user receives mu already evaluated observed time points. user therefore needs supply Y_mat ~ 1 data frame matrix-valued response. basis construction, FPCA pre-fit, centering required caller.

title: Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis
text: simulated data set dat contains: Y_mat: n×Mn \times M matrix-valued column functional observations, inx: dummy index column (present data frame right number rows; used model).
code: set.seed(12345) library(refundBayes) library(refund) library(ggplot2)  ## Dimensions n     <- 200 M     <- 50 tgrid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M)  ## True mean function and eigenfunctions mu_true <- sin(pi * tgrid) phi1    <- sqrt(2) * sin(2 * pi * tgrid) phi2    <- sqrt(2) * cos(2 * pi * tgrid) phi_true <- rbind(phi1, phi2)            # J x M eigen_true     <- c(2, 0.5) sigma_eps_true <- 0.3  ## Simulate scores and observations xi_true <- cbind(rnorm(n, sd = sqrt(eigen_true[1])),                  rnorm(n, sd = sqrt(eigen_true[2]))) Y_mat <- matrix(rep(mu_true, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE) +          xi_true %*% phi_true +          matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = sigma_eps_true), nrow = n)  dat <- data.frame(inx = 1:n) dat$Y_mat <- Y_mat

text: Posterior summaries can computed directly returned arrays:
code: ## Posterior mean of the mean function on the input grid mu_hat <- apply(fit_fpca$mu, 2, mean)  ## Pointwise 95% credible interval for the mean function mu_lo  <- apply(fit_fpca$mu, 2, function(x) quantile(x, 0.025)) mu_hi  <- apply(fit_fpca$mu, 2, function(x) quantile(x, 0.975))  ## Posterior mean of the FPC eigenvalue SDs lambda_hat <- apply(fit_fpca$evalues, 2, mean)  ## Posterior mean of the FPC scores (n x J matrix of point estimates) scores_hat <- apply(fit_fpca$scores, c(2, 3), mean)  ## Posterior mean of the residual SD sigma_eps_hat <- mean(fit_fpca$sigma)

title: Joint FPCA Modeling in refundBayes
text: vignette describes joint FPCA option available across refundBayes regression functions: sofr_bayes() – Bayesian Scalar--Function Regression, fcox_bayes() – Bayesian Functional Cox Regression, fofr_bayes() – Bayesian Function--Function Regression. every case, option exposed via joint_FPCA argument: logical (TRUE / FALSE) vector length equal number functional predictors. ii-th entry TRUE, observed functional predictor Wi(s)W_i(s) used directly covariate; instead, regression model built top functional principal component analysis (FPCA) sub-model Wi(s)W_i(s), FPC scores sampled jointly regression coefficients. methodology mirrors Section 4 Jiang, Crainiceanu, Cui (2025), Tutorial Bayesian Functional Regression Using Stan, Statistics Medicine 44(20–22), e70265. default option (joint_FPCA = NULL) adopt joint modelling.

title: Joint FPCA Modeling in refundBayes
text: standard scalar--function functional Cox regression, integral ∫Wi(s)β(s)ds\int W_i(s)\beta(s)\,ds plugged Wi(s)W_i(s) observed without error. practice, curves sampled finitely many points typically corrupted measurement noise. Treating Wi(s)W_i(s) exact ignores uncertainty biases regression coefficient β(⋅)\beta(\cdot) toward zero (classical errors--variables attenuation), underestimates posterior credible-interval width. joint FPCA approach replaces Wi(s)W_i(s) low-rank FPCA representation Wi(s)=μ(s)+∑j=1Jξijϕj(s)+ϵi(s),W_i(s) \;=\; \mu(s) \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{J} \xi_{ij}\,\phi_j(s) \;+\; \epsilon_i(s), treats subject-specific scores ξij\xi_{ij} parameters sampled together regression coefficients. FPC eigenfunctions ϕj(s)\phi_j(s) estimated refund::fpca.sc() held fixed, initial frequentist FPCA scores ξ̂ij\hat\xi_{ij} used prior mean ξij\xi_{ij}. propagates FPCA uncertainty posterior β(⋅)\beta(\cdot), regression credible bands correctly inflated.

what: The Joint Bayesian Model We Adopted

text: model two layers share FPC scores ξij\xi_{ij}: FPCA likelihood observed functional predictor; outcome regression (SoFR / FCox / FoFR), scores ξij\xi_{ij} enter linear predictor. text: complete joint model (one functional predictor) reads {𝐎𝐮𝐭𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐫𝐞𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧:ηi=η0+𝐙𝛄+𝛏(𝐗̃rt𝐛̃r+𝐗̃ft𝐛̃f)𝐅𝐏𝐂𝐀 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐡𝐨𝐨𝐝:Wi(sm)∣𝛏,𝚽,σe∼N(∑jξijϕj(sm),σe2)𝐅𝐏𝐂 𝐬𝐜𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐨𝐫:ξij∼N(ξ̂ij,λj2)𝐇𝐲𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐨𝐫𝐬:𝐛̃r∼N(𝟎,σb2𝐈),σb2,σe2,λj2∼Inv-Gamma(0.001,0.001)𝐎𝐮𝐭𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐛𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧:Yi∼p(Yi∣ηi)(Gaussian / binomial / Cox / functional)\begin{cases} \textbf{Outcome regression}\colon\quad \eta_i = \eta_0 + \mathbf{Z}_i^{t}\boldsymbol\gamma + \boldsymbol\xi_i^{t}\!\left(\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_r^{t}\,\tilde{\mathbf{b}}_r + \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_f^{t}\,\tilde{\mathbf{b}}_f\right) \\[6pt] \textbf{FPCA likelihood}\colon\quad W_i(s_m) \mid \boldsymbol\xi_i, \boldsymbol\Phi, \sigma_e \sim N\!\left(\sum_j \xi_{ij}\phi_j(s_m),\,\sigma_e^2\right) \\[4pt] \textbf{FPC score prior}\colon\quad \xi_{ij} \sim N(\hat\xi_{ij},\,\lambda_j^{2}) \\[4pt] \textbf{Hyperpriors}\colon\quad \tilde{\mathbf{b}}_r \sim N(\mathbf{0},\sigma_b^2\mathbf{}),\;\; \sigma_b^2,\,\sigma_e^2,\,\lambda_j^{2} \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(0.001, 0.001) \\[4pt] \textbf{Outcome distribution}\colon\quad Y_i \sim p(Y_i \mid \eta_i)\quad\text{(Gaussian / binomial / Cox / functional)} \end{cases} four building blocks (outcome regression, FPCA likelihood, score prior, hyperpriors) independent family; outcome distribution last line changes. precisely joint FPCA option available across SoFR, FCox, FoFR joint_FPCA argument.

code: set.seed(123) n <- 100 M <- 50 tgrid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M) dt    <- tgrid[2] - tgrid[1] tmat  <- matrix(rep(tgrid, each = n), nrow = n) lmat  <- matrix(dt, nrow = n, ncol = M)  # Smooth latent trajectory + measurement noise on the functional predictor D_true <- t(apply(matrix(rnorm(n * M), n, M), 1, cumsum)) / sqrt(M) wmat   <- D_true + matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = 0.3), nrow = n) ## noisy  beta_true <- sin(2 * pi * tgrid) X1        <- rnorm(n) eta       <- 0.5 * X1 + D_true %*% (beta_true * dt)   ## regression on D, not W prob      <- plogis(eta) y         <- rbinom(n, 1, prob)  data.SoFR <- data.frame(y = y, X1 = X1) data.SoFR$tmat <- tmat data.SoFR$lmat <- lmat data.SoFR$wmat <- wmat

what: Simulated FoFR with measurement error on the predictor
title: Joint FPCA Modeling in refundBayes
code: library(refundBayes) set.seed(42)  n <- 200 L <- 30 M <- 30 sindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = L) tindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M)  # Smooth latent functional predictor + measurement noise D_true <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = L) for (i in 1:n) {   D_true[i, ] <- rnorm(1) * sin(2 * pi * sindex) +                  rnorm(1) * cos(2 * pi * sindex) +                  rnorm(1) * sin(4 * pi * sindex) } X_func <- D_true + matrix(rnorm(n * L, sd = 0.3), nrow = n)   ## noisy  age <- rnorm(n)  # True coefficient functions beta_true  <- outer(sin(2 * pi * sindex), cos(2 * pi * tindex)) alpha_true <- 0.5 * sin(pi * tindex)  # Generate response from the latent D_true (not from X_func!) signal_scalar <- outer(age, alpha_true) signal_func   <- (D_true %*% beta_true) / L epsilon       <- matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = 0.3), nrow = n) Y_mat         <- signal_scalar + signal_func + epsilon  dat <- data.frame(age = age) dat$Y_mat  <- Y_mat dat$X_func <- X_func dat$sindex <- matrix(rep(sindex, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE)

title: Bayesian Scalar-on-Function Regression with `refundBayes::sofr_bayes`
text: Scalar--Function Regression (SoFR) models relationship scalar outcome one functional predictors (curves trajectories observed continuum), along optional scalar covariates. subject =1,…,ni = 1, \ldots, n, let YiY_i scalar outcome, 𝐙\mathbf{Z}_i p×1p \times 1 vector scalar predictors, {Wi(tm),tm∈𝒯}\{W_i(t_m), t_m \\mathcal{T}\} m=1,…,Mm = 1, \ldots, M functional predictor observed MM time points domain 𝒯\mathcal{T}. SoFR model assumes distribution YiY_i belongs exponential family mean μi\mu_i, linear predictor ηi=g(μi)\eta_i = g(\mu_i) following structure: ηi=η0+∫𝒯Wi(t)β(t)dt+𝐙𝛄\eta_i = \eta_0 + \int_{\mathcal{T}} W_i(t)\beta(t)\,dt + \mathbf{Z}_i^t \boldsymbol{\gamma} : η0\eta_0 overall intercept, β(⋅)∈L2(𝒯)\beta(\cdot) \L^2(\mathcal{T}) unknown functional coefficient characterizes effect functional predictor outcome, 𝛄\boldsymbol{\gamma} p×1p \times 1 vector scalar regression coefficients, 𝒯\mathcal{T} domain functional predictor. domain restricted [0,1][0,1]; determined actual time points data (e.g., 𝒯=[1,1440]\mathcal{T} = [1, 1440] minute-level 24-hour activity data, 𝒯=[0,100]\mathcal{T} = [0, 100] generic index set). integral ∫𝒯Wi(t)β(t)dt\int_{\mathcal{T}} W_i(t)\beta(t)\,dt approximated using Riemann sum observed time points.

title: Bayesian Scalar-on-Function Regression with `refundBayes::sofr_bayes`
text: example dataset data.SoFR contains: y: binary outcome variable, X1: scalar predictor, tmat: n×Mn \times M time point matrix (defines domain 𝒯\mathcal{T}), lmat: n×Mn \times M Riemann sum weight matrix (defines integration weights 𝒯\mathcal{T}), wmat: n×Mn \times M functional predictor matrix.
code: ## Alternative sample data   # data.SoFR <- readRDS("data/example_data_sofr.rds")  ## Load the example data set.seed(123) n <- 100 M <- 50 tgrid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M) dt    <- tgrid[2] - tgrid[1] tmat  <- matrix(rep(tgrid, each = n), nrow = n) lmat  <- matrix(dt, nrow = n, ncol = M) wmat  <- t(apply(matrix(rnorm(n * M), n, M), 1, cumsum)) / sqrt(M) beta_true <- sin(2 * pi * tgrid) X1 <- rnorm(n) eta <- 0.5 * X1 + wmat %*% (beta_true * dt) prob <- plogis(eta) y <- rbinom(n, 1, prob) data.SoFR <- data.frame(y = y, X1 = X1) data.SoFR$tmat <- tmat data.SoFR$lmat <- lmat data.SoFR$wmat <- wmat

title: Bayesian Scalar-on-Function Regression with `refundBayes::sofr_bayes`
text: call: formula specifies binary outcome y one scalar predictor X1 one functional predictor encoded via s(tmat, = lmat * wmat, bs = "cc", k = 10). spline basis evaluated time points stored tmat, integration domain 𝒯\mathcal{T} (determined tmat) approximated using weights lmat. bs = "cc" uses cyclic cubic regression splines, appropriate functional predictor periodic (e.g., physical activity measured 24-hour cycle). k = 10 specifies 10 basis functions. practice, 30–40 basis functions often sufficient moderately smooth functional data dense grids. family = binomial() specifies logistic regression binary outcome. sampler runs 3 chains parallel, 1500 total iterations (500 warmup + 1000 posterior samples).
code: library(refundBayes)  refundBayes_SoFR <- refundBayes::sofr_bayes(   y ~ X1 + s(tmat, by = lmat * wmat, bs = "cc", k = 10),   data = data.SoFR,   family = binomial(),   runStan = TRUE,   niter = 1500,   nwarmup = 500,   nchain = 3,   ncores = 3 )

title: Bayesian Scalar-on-Function Regression with `refundBayes::sofr_bayes`
text: Number basis functions (k): illustrative purposes, k = 10 often used. practice, 30–40 basis functions recommended moderately smooth functional data observed dense grids. Spline type (bs): Use "cr" (cubic regression splines) general functional predictors. Use "cc" (cyclic cubic regression splines) functional predictor periodic (e.g., 24-hour activity patterns). Number iterations: Ensure sufficient posterior samples reliable inference. common setup niter = 3000 nwarmup = 1000, complex models may require additional iterations. Convergence diagnostics: fitting, examine traceplots R̂\hat{R} statistics using rstan package (e.g., rstan::traceplot(refundBayes_SoFR$stanfit)) ensure Markov chains converged. Joint FPCA: functional predictors measured substantial noise, consider setting joint_FPCA = TRUE relevant predictor jointly estimate FPCA scores regression coefficients. See Joint FPCA vignette details.

title: refundBayes — Bayesian Regression with Functional Data
text: example fits Bayesian Scalar--Function Regression (SoFR) model bundled example_data_sofr dataset, contains scalar outcome y, scalar predictor X1, one functional predictor stored three matched n x M matrices: tmat (observation times), lmat (Riemann-sum integration weights L_m = t_{m+1} - t_m), wmat (functional predictor values W_i(t_m)).
code: library(refundBayes)  ## Load the bundled example dataset (see ?example_data_sofr for variable details) data(example_data_sofr)  ## Fit a Bayesian Scalar-on-Function Regression model. ## Formula syntax mirrors mgcv::gam: ##   - X1                              : scalar covariate (linear effect) ##   - s(tmat, by = lmat * wmat, ...)  : functional term encoding the integral ##                                       int_T W_i(t) * beta(t) dt as a Riemann ##                                       sum over the time grid in `tmat`, ##                                       weighted by `lmat * wmat` ##   - bs = "cr"                       : cubic regression spline basis for beta(t) ##                                       (use "cc" for periodic predictors, e.g. ##                                       24-hour activity profiles) ##   - k  = 10                         : number of spline basis functions ##                                       (30-40 is recommended in practice) fit <- sofr_bayes(   y ~ X1 + s(tmat, by = lmat * wmat, bs = "cr", k = 10),   data    = example_data_sofr,   family  = gaussian(),  # outcome distribution; binomial() is also supported   niter   = 2000,        # total HMC iterations per chain (warmup + sampling)   nwarmup = 1000,        # warmup (burn-in) iterations, discarded for inference   nchain  = 1            # number of chains; use >= 3 in practice for diagnostics )  ## `fit` is an object of class "refundBayes" containing posterior samples for ## the intercept, scalar coefficient(s), and the functional coefficient beta(t). summary(fit)   # posterior summaries (mean, SD, 95% CrI) for all parameters plot(fit)      # estimated beta(t) with pointwise 95% credible band

title: Bayesian Functional Cox Regression — fcox_bayes
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate survival data with a functional predictor set.seed(1) n  <- 150  # number of subjects L  <- 50   # number of functional domain points Lindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = L)       # functional domain grid X_func <- matrix(rnorm(n * L), nrow = n)  # functional predictor (n x L) age    <- rnorm(n)                         # scalar predictor  # True functional effect and linear predictor beta_true <- cos(pi * Lindex) lp <- X_func %*% beta_true / L + age  # Generate survival times from an exponential baseline hazard time  <- rexp(n, rate = exp(lp)) cens_time <- runif(n, min = 0.5, max = 3) obs_time  <- pmin(time, cens_time) cens_ind  <- as.integer(time <= cens_time)  # 1 = event, 0 = censored  dat <- data.frame(obs_time = obs_time, age = age) dat$X_func <- X_func dat$Lindex <- matrix(rep(Lindex, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE)  # Fit the Bayesian Functional Cox model fit_cox <- fcox_bayes(   formula = obs_time ~ age + s(Lindex, by = X_func, bs = "cr", k = 10),   data    = dat,   cens    = cens_ind,   niter   = 2000,   nwarmup = 1000,   nchain  = 3 )  # Summarise scalar coefficients and plot functional coefficient summary(fit_cox) plot(fit_cox) } # }

title: Bayesian Function-on-Function Regression — fofr_bayes
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data for a Function-on-Function Regression model set.seed(1) n  <- 100  # number of subjects L  <- 30   # number of functional predictor domain points M  <- 30   # number of functional response domain points sindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = L)  # predictor domain grid tindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M)  # response domain grid  # Functional predictor X_func <- matrix(rnorm(n * L), nrow = n)  # Scalar predictor age <- rnorm(n)  # True bivariate coefficient beta(s, t) beta_true <- outer(sin(2 * pi * sindex), cos(2 * pi * tindex))  # True scalar coefficient function alpha_true <- sin(pi * tindex)  # Generate functional response: Y(t) = age * alpha(t) + integral X(s) beta(s,t) ds + error Y_mat <- outer(age, alpha_true) + X_func %*% beta_true / L +           matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = 0.3), nrow = n)  dat <- data.frame(age = age) dat$Y_mat  <- Y_mat dat$X_func <- X_func dat$sindex <- matrix(rep(sindex, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE)  # Fit the Bayesian FoFR model fit_fofr <- fofr_bayes(   formula    = Y_mat ~ age + s(sindex, by = X_func, bs = "cr", k = 10),   data       = dat,   spline_type = "bs",   spline_df  = 10,   niter      = 2000,   nwarmup    = 1000,   nchain     = 3 )  # Examine the bivariate coefficient beta(s, t) (posterior mean) beta_est <- apply(fit_fofr$bivar_func_coef[[1]], c(2, 3), mean) image(sindex, tindex, beta_est,       xlab = "s (predictor domain)", ylab = "t (response domain)",       main = expression(hat(beta)(s, t)))  # Fit a FoFR model with functional predictors only (no scalar predictors) fit_fofr2 <- fofr_bayes(   formula    = Y_mat ~ s(sindex, by = X_func, bs = "cr", k = 10),   data       = dat,   niter      = 2000,   nwarmup    = 1000,   nchain     = 3 ) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data for a Function-on-Scalar Regression model set.seed(1) n  <- 100   # number of subjects M  <- 50    # number of functional response observation points tindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M)  # response functional domain grid  # Scalar predictors age <- rnorm(n) sex <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # True coefficient functions beta_age <- sin(2 * pi * tindex) beta_sex <- cos(2 * pi * tindex)  # Generate functional response (n x M matrix) epsilon  <- matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = 0.3), nrow = n) Y_mat    <- outer(age, beta_age) + outer(sex, beta_sex) + epsilon  dat <- data.frame(age = age, sex = sex) dat$Y_mat <- Y_mat  # Fit the Bayesian FoSR model fit_fosr <- fosr_bayes(   formula    = Y_mat ~ age + sex,   data       = dat,   spline_type = "bs",   spline_df  = 10,   niter      = 2000,   nwarmup    = 1000,   nchain     = 3 )  # Plot estimated coefficient functions plot(fit_fosr) } # }

title: Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis — fpca_bayes
text: Bayesian FPCA model implemented following tutorial Jiang et al., 2025. model decomposes dense functional response \(Y_i(t)\) smooth population mean function \(\mu(t)\) sum functional principal components, $$Y_i(t) = \mu(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \xi_{ik} \phi_k(t) + \epsilon_i(t),$$ \(\phi_k(t)\) orthonormal eigenfunctions obtained initial frequentist FPCA fit (used fixed basis), mean function \(\mu(t)\), FPC scores \(\xi_{ik}\), eigenvalues \(\lambda_k\), residual variance estimated via posterior sampling. population mean function modelled penalized spline using syntax R mgcv package.

title: Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis — fpca_bayes
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate functional data with two underlying principal components set.seed(1) n  <- 100   # number of subjects M  <- 50    # number of functional observation points tindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = M)  # True mean function and eigenfunctions mu_true <- sin(pi * tindex) phi1    <- sqrt(2) * sin(2 * pi * tindex) phi2    <- sqrt(2) * cos(2 * pi * tindex)  # Simulate scores and noisy observations xi1 <- rnorm(n, 0, sqrt(2)) xi2 <- rnorm(n, 0, sqrt(0.5)) Y_mat <- matrix(rep(mu_true, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE) +          outer(xi1, phi1) + outer(xi2, phi2) +          matrix(rnorm(n * M, sd = 0.3), nrow = n)  dat <- data.frame(inx = 1:n) dat$Y_mat <- Y_mat  # Fit the Bayesian FPCA model fit_fpca <- fpca_bayes(   formula     = Y_mat ~ 1,   data        = dat,   spline_type = "bs",   spline_df   = 10,   niter       = 2000,   nwarmup     = 1000,   nchain      = 3 )  # Posterior mean of the mean function mu_est <- apply(fit_fpca$mu, 2, mean) plot(tindex, mu_est, type = "l", ylab = expression(hat(mu)(t)))  # Posterior means of the FPC scores and eigenvalues scores_est  <- apply(fit_fpca$scores, c(2, 3), mean) evalues_est <- apply(fit_fpca$evalues, 2, mean) } # }

title: Bayesian Scalar-on-Function Regression — sofr_bayes
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data for a Gaussian SoFR model set.seed(1) n  <- 100  # number of subjects L  <- 50   # number of functional domain points Lindex <- seq(0, 1, length.out = L)       # functional domain grid X_func <- matrix(rnorm(n * L), nrow = n)  # functional predictor (n x L) age    <- rnorm(n)                         # scalar predictor beta_true <- sin(pi * Lindex)         # true functional coefficient eta <- X_func %*% beta_true / L Y <- eta + 0.5 * age + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)  dat <- data.frame(Y = Y, age = age) dat$X_func  <- X_func dat$Lindex  <- matrix(rep(Lindex, n), nrow = n, byrow = TRUE)  # Fit Gaussian SoFR fit_sofr <- sofr_bayes(   formula = Y ~ age + s(Lindex, by = X_func, bs = "cr", k = 10),   data    = dat,   family  = "gaussian",   niter   = 2000,   nwarmup = 1000,   nchain  = 3 )  # Summarise and plot estimated functional coefficient summary(fit_sofr) plot(fit_sofr)  # Fit binomial SoFR prob <- plogis(X_func %*% beta_true / L) Y_bin <- rbinom(n, 1, prob) dat$Y_bin <- Y_bin fit_bin <- sofr_bayes(   formula = Y_bin ~ s(Lindex, by = X_func, bs = "cr", k = 10),   data    = dat,   family  = "binomial",   niter   = 2000,   nwarmup = 1000,   nchain  = 3 ) } # }

title: refundBayes 0.6.0
text: Added fpca_bayes() Bayesian Functional Principal Component Analysis, modelling functional outcome μ(t) plus low-rank FPC expansion posterior inference mean function, FPC scores, eigenvalue standard deviations, residual SD. Initial eigenfunctions obtained refund::fpca.sc() held fixed sampling. Added joint_FPCA argument sofr_bayes(), fcox_bayes(), fofr_bayes() jointly modelling functional predictor via FPCA alongside regression coefficients. enabled, predictor replaced FPCA representation FPC scores sampled jointly β(·), propagating measurement-error uncertainty posterior regression coefficient (errors--variables-aware fit).

title: refundBayes 0.5.0
text: sofr_bayes() — Bayesian Scalar--Function Regression, supporting Gaussian, binomial, Poisson families, one functional predictors alongside scalar covariates. fosr_bayes() — Bayesian Function--Scalar Regression FPCA-based residual structure modelling subject-level functional deviations. fcox_bayes() — Bayesian Functional Cox Regression time--event outcomes functional scalar predictors, including posterior inference log-hazard ratio surface.

title: refundBayes 0.5.0
text: Functional coefficients represented via spline bases constructed mgcv::smoothCon(), spectral reparameterisation (mgcv::smooth2random()) fixed random effect components. Non-centered parameterisation penalised spline coefficients efficient HMC sampling. Inverse-gamma priors smoothing variance components weakly informative priors fixed-effect coefficients. Support multiple spline bases (cubic regression, thin-plate, P-spline, etc.) via bs argument formula interface. Generic summary() plot() methods posterior summaries visualisation functional coefficients credible bands.
